Approach 1: MasTec Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what the future stream of cash is worth in present day dollars.

For MasTec, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach that starts from last twelve months free cash flow of about $435.8 million. Analysts provide cash flow estimates for the earlier years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further out, with projected free cash flow of $1.1b in 2030. These projections are all treated in dollar terms, even if reporting and listing currencies differ.

When all projected cash flows are discounted and added up, the DCF model points to an estimated intrinsic value of about $233.85 per share, compared with the recent share price of $241.80. That implies MasTec screens as roughly 3.4% overvalued on this method, which is a fairly small gap rather than a glaring mispricing.

For a profitable company like MasTec, the P/E ratio is a common yardstick because it links what you are paying directly to the earnings the business is generating today. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends a lot on how the market sees the company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower multiple.

MasTec currently trades on a P/E of 56.71x. That sits above the Construction industry average of 37.08x and above the peer group average of 49.96x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for MasTec is 35.19x. data.title: Red Cat’s Revenue Surge Tests Durability Of Defense Drone Opportunity
data.description:
• Red Cat Holdings (NasdaqCM:RCAT) preannounced Q4 sales that point to a sharp jump in revenue tied to its domestically manufactured drones.
  • The company is highlighting expansion opportunities as U.S. regulations restrict foreign-made drones in sensitive use cases.
  • Recent partnerships with Palantir and Safe Pro are aimed at broadening Red Cat's role within defense and government drone programs.
  • Heightened geopolitical tensions are contributing to stronger demand signals from defense buyers for Red Cat's drone solutions.

Red Cat Holdings focuses on U.S. made drone systems, which positions it directly in the flow of regulatory changes that favor domestic suppliers. With new restrictions on foreign-made drones in certain government and defense settings, investors are watching how this backdrop intersects with the Q4 sales surge that management has flagged.

For investors, a central consideration is how durable this momentum could be if current demand patterns from defense and government customers continue. The company’s relationships with partners such as Palantir and Safe Pro may influence how much of that demand Red Cat can capture, and how its role in the broader defense drone market evolves over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Red Cat Holdings by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Red Cat Holdings.

Why Red Cat Holdings could be great value

Red Cat’s Q4 2025 revenue guidance of US$24.0m to US$26.5m, compared with US$1.3m a year earlier, signals a step change in scale rather than a small incremental shift. Full year guidance of US$38.0m to US$41.0m versus US$15.6m in 2024, together with commentary about production ramp up and U.S. regulations favoring domestic drones, appears to be feeding into the recent investor interest and analyst target revisions.

Advertisement

How This Fits The Red Cat Holdings Narrative

Recent analyst commentary describing Red Cat as “uniquely positioned” in unmanned systems and pointing to contracts with the U.S. Army aligns with the sharp revenue guidance increase and the FCC ban on foreign drones and parts. At the same time, a high level of short interest and references to valuation and execution risk indicate that not all investors are convinced the current growth and opportunity set will translate smoothly into long term results.

Red Cat Holdings, Balancing Rewards And Risks

  • Very large guided revenue step up for Q4 and strong full year growth expectations suggest that the company is scaling into larger defense and government programs.
  • Partnerships with Palantir and Safe Pro, plus new product areas such as the Blue Ops maritime division, broaden the potential use cases for Red Cat’s drones and services.
  • Analysts have highlighted that Red Cat is currently unprofitable and not forecast to reach profitability within 3 years, which can keep execution risk front of mind.
  • Short interest of 22.26% of float indicates a sizeable group of investors positioning for setbacks or a pullback if expectations are not met.

What To Watch Next

Looking ahead, you may want to follow how actual Q4 results compare with guidance, whether new defense orders or certifications are announced, and how short interest and analyst views shift around events such as the February Innovation Day. Check how other investors are interpreting these signals and how they are building their Red Cat Holdings narrative to put this news in broader context.

• If you are wondering whether Madrigal Pharmaceuticals at around US$480 a share is still priced attractively, you are not alone. This article focuses squarely on what that price might mean for value focused investors.
  • The stock has had a mixed run, with a 46.4% return over 1 year and a very large 5 year gain, while shorter term moves have been weaker, including a 4.2% decline over 7 days, 19.8% over 30 days, and 19.1% year to date.
  • Recent news coverage has centered on Madrigal's position in the pharmaceuticals and biotech space and the market's reaction to company specific developments such as clinical progress and regulatory milestones. This backdrop helps explain why the share price has seen both strong multi year returns and more recent pullbacks as expectations and perceived risks are reassessed.
  • Right now, Madrigal scores a 5/6 valuation check score. Next, we will look at how different methods like multiples and discounted cash flows assess that, before finishing with a framework that can help you think about valuation in a more complete way.

Approach 1: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today, aiming to estimate what the entire business might be worth in present dollar terms.

For Madrigal Pharmaceuticals, the model used is a 2 stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of about US$164.0 million, but analysts and extrapolated estimates in this model point to positive free cash flow ahead. The projections provided reach around US$1.78b of free cash flow in 2030, with interim annual figures between 2026 and 2035 discounted back to today using Simply Wall St’s assumptions.

Putting all those projected and discounted cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$1,834.38 per share. Compared with the current share price of roughly US$480, the DCF output for this model suggests Madrigal could be about 73.8% undervalued on this set of assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are not yet positive, the P/S ratio is often a useful gauge because it compares the market value to the revenue the business is already generating, without relying on profit figures that can still be negative during heavy investment phases.

What counts as a reasonable P/S ratio depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” P/S, while slower growth or higher uncertainty usually point to a lower one.

Madrigal currently trades on a P/S ratio of about 14.73x. That sits above the Biotechs industry average of roughly 11.90x and below the peer group average of around 23.79x. Simply Wall St also provides a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for Madrigal of 20.17x, which reflects factors such as its growth profile, risk characteristics, margins, size and industry.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight comparison with peers or the industry because it aims to align the multiple with Madrigal’s specific fundamentals rather than broad group averages. With the current P/S at 14.73x versus a Fair Ratio of 20.17x, the shares screen as undervalued on this metric.

data.title: Constellation Energy’s Calpine Deal And Tech Nuclear Pacts Reframe Growth Potential
data.description:
• Constellation Energy has completed its acquisition of Calpine, making it the largest electricity producer in the U.S.
  • The company has signed 20 year nuclear power supply agreements with major tech companies, including Microsoft and Meta Platforms.
  • A new federal policy now requires Big Tech to directly fund new power plants, reshaping how large power projects may be financed.

Constellation Energy (NasdaqGS:CEG) now combines Calpine's natural gas and geothermal fleet with its existing nuclear assets, giving it a scale that few peers can match in serving power hungry customers. The stock closed at $289.06, with a 3 year return of about 3.4x and a 1 year decline of 16.3%, so you are looking at a name that has already moved significantly and has recently pulled back.

For investors watching the growth of data centers and artificial intelligence, these long term deals with Microsoft and Meta, along with new rules that push Big Tech to fund new plants, put Constellation at the center of how future power capacity could be built. The key question from here is how the company structures contracts and capital commitments so that it can support large scale projects while managing risk for existing shareholders.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Constellation Energy by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Constellation Energy.

Why Constellation Energy could be great value

The Calpine deal and the long dated contracts with Microsoft and Meta signal that Constellation is shifting further toward being a preferred partner for the data economy rather than a pure wholesale power trader. The sell off after the federal move to make Big Tech fund new plants shows that some investors worry policy could limit returns on the existing fleet, even as the company lines up customers that want exactly the kind of nuclear heavy, 24/7 power supply it offers.

Advertisement

Constellation Energy narrative, tested but still front and center

For many investors the core story here has been that Constellation sits at the intersection of rising AI and data center demand and a large nuclear footprint, supported by long term contracts like the 20 year deals with Meta and Microsoft. The new policy requirement for tech to underwrite new plants does not remove that story. However, it shifts attention to how much of future value might come from new build projects versus price exposure on already operating assets.

Risks and rewards in focus

  • 🎁 Long term agreements with Microsoft and Meta provide clearer visibility on future cash flows tied to specific nuclear assets.
  • 🎁 The Calpine acquisition, expected to add about US$2b of annual free cash flow, gives Constellation more scale and fuel diversity when dealing with large power buyers.
  • ⚠️ Policy efforts to cap prices and push Big Tech to fund new capacity could limit upside from selling existing nuclear output at high market rates.
  • ⚠️ The stock has already delivered roughly 3.4x over 3 years and remains volatile, so sentiment swings around regulation and AI demand may continue to be sharp.

What to watch next

From here, watch how Constellation structures new contracts under the Big Tech funding rules, how Calpine integration affects its cash generation, and whether further nuclear or gas projects are backed by similar long term deals. If you want to see how other investors are thinking about these shifts, you can check out community narratives by heading over to this discussion hub.
